---
title: "When Experts Disagree, Where Do You Look First?"
subtitle: "One carbohydrate food can earn five expert verdicts. Here is the tool we built to make sense of them — and to find the disagreements worth resolving."
author:
  - "Josh Erndt-Marino, PhD"
  - "FRESH's AI assistant"
date: "2026-06-20"
description: |
  Expert food-rating systems disagree — whole-wheat bread scores anywhere from
  the 12th to the 99th percentile depending on whom you ask. The meta-NPS
  harmonizes them into a single healthfulness–impact map. Watch it build itself,
  then explore.
categories: [meta-NPS, carbohydrate-quality, IAFNS, FRESH, interactive]
resources:
  - story.html
---

Ask five expert panels whether whole-wheat bread is healthy and you can get five
different answers — from the bottom tenth of the food supply to the very top.
That is not a measurement error. It is what happens when serious people
operationalize the word *healthy* five different ways.

So in the [IAFNS Carbohydrate Quality series](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=ekCUfds-sWQ)
I built a **meta–nutritional profiling system** — a tool that doesn't pick a
winner among the rating systems, but harmonizes them: their middle becomes a
starting score (the *meta-score*), the foods the experts can't agree on are
penalized for it, and every food lands on one map.

The chart below **teaches itself**. Let it run — it starts with a single loaf and
its five verdicts, shows how they become one penalized score, orients each axis
with examples, then fills in the map and its regions. Then it hands the map to you.

```{=html}
<iframe id="storyFrame" class="story-frame" src="story.html?v=23" title="Interactive: the healthfulness–impact map building itself"
        style="width:100%;border:1px solid #eee8df;border-radius:12px;background:#fffdf8;height:620px"
        scrolling="no"></iframe>
<script>
(function(){
  var f=document.getElementById("storyFrame");
  function fit(){ try{ var d=f.contentDocument; if(d&&d.body){ f.style.height=(d.body.scrollHeight+8)+"px"; } }catch(e){} }
  f.addEventListener("load",function(){ fit(); setTimeout(fit,400); });
  window.addEventListener("resize",fit);
  // the story posts "story-fit" whenever it reflows (resize); re-fit then too
  window.addEventListener("message",function(e){ if(e&&e.data==="story-fit"){ fit(); } });
  // tell the embedded story to play once it scrolls into the article's view
  if("IntersectionObserver" in window){
    var io=new IntersectionObserver(function(es){
      es.forEach(function(en){ if(en.isIntersecting){ try{ f.contentWindow.postMessage("play-story","*"); }catch(e){} } });
    }, {threshold:0.25});
    io.observe(f);
  }
})();
</script>
```

## What you just watched

Each food's vertical position is its **Food Recommendation Index** — the
meta-system's single read on healthfulness, which rewards a high median score
*and* agreement between systems. A food with a respectable median can still be
pulled down when the experts split on it. Whole-wheat bread is exactly that: its
median lands in the low 70s, but its systems range from 12 to 99, so it settles
at 53, below where the median alone would put it.

Two foods can share that middle band for opposite reasons — **neutral** (the
experts agree it is middling) or **uncertain** (they don't). Solid dots are the
agreers; hollow dots are the split. That distinction is the whole point: it
separates "we know this food is so-so" from "we don't actually know."

The horizontal position is **impact** — how much of it America actually eats —
and this is the lever. A food the experts can't agree on barely matters if no one
eats it; a *contested staple* is exactly where harmonizing the science would move
real diets. So the foods worth a panel's time sit to the **right and hollow**:
widely eaten *and* genuinely uncertain. That is where you look first.

One caveat: the quiet, low-impact corner is not worthless. Foods few people eat
are often where the *method* gets tested and the theory sharpened — they teach us
how the rating systems behave, even when fixing a single score wouldn't move the
needle on public health. Impact tells you where to **act**; the whole map is
where you **learn**.

::: {.callout-note appearance="simple"}
Every dot now carries its real meta-NPS values: the five expert scores, the
meta-score, and the FRI, computed across the US food supply (about 6,500 foods in
the FNDDS database). The left–right axis is real consumption too. Of the roughly
3,200 carb-rich foods Americans actually eat, the 122 most commonly consumed
account for about half of all US carbohydrate intake, and the
[webinar tool](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=ekCUfds-sWQ) maps those 122. This
interactive plots a recognizable subset of them so the idea stays legible; the
full tool and dataset sit behind the [meta-NPS paper](https://doi.org/10.1080/09637486.2023.2241672).
:::

This is the first of the carbohydrate-quality tools. The
[next webinar](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=hjKmtaeRKLc) uses this same map to
ask where the disagreements actually cluster — and the answer turns out to be
"more places than you'd hope." See all of them on the
[resources page](../resources/).
